original_abstract: Psychological maturity for marriage remains a critical predictor for the stability and quality of future family life, particularly in an era of shifting social attitudes toward marital permanence. This study examines the relationship between psychological maturity for marriage and emotional intelligence among young men. To investigate this link, a study was conducted with 90 men aged 21 to 26, using the Psychological Maturity for Marriage Scale (SKALDOM II) and the Emotional Intelligence Questionnaire (INTE). Psychological maturity was assessed across three dimensions: self-acceptance, internal integration and world view, and attitudes toward marriage and family life. Comparative analysis of groups with high and low maturity scores revealed that men with higher psychological maturity possess significantly higher levels of emotional intelligence. Specifically, these individuals demonstrate a superior capacity for self-awareness, empathy, and the constructive regulation of emotions during problem-solving and social interactions. The findings suggest that emotional intelligence serves as a foundational competency for building resilient interpersonal bonds. The paper concludes by emphasizing the value of fostering emotional competencies through educational and family-oriented programs to better prepare young men for the complexities of modern marital and family life.
humanized_abstract: Psychological maturity for marriage remains to be an important predictor for future family life. As the conditions for marriage are developing, it is vital that young couples who plan to live together for a long period of time are adequately prepared for their challenging roles as parents. In this study the relationship between psychological maturity for marriage and emotional intelligence in young men is investigated. 90 men, between the ages of 21 and 26 years, took part in this study. They completed the Psychological Maturity for Marriage Scale (SKALDOM II) and the Emotional Intelligence Questionnaire (INTE). This scale includes three components: self-acceptance, internal integration and world view, and attitudes toward marriage and family life. The men who scored high in the SKALDOM II were compared with men who scored low on this scale and it was shown that men with high psychological maturity for marriage had a significantly higher score on emotional intelligence then men with low maturity. The emotional competencies with the highest scores were self awareness, sensitivity for emotions of others and skill to control own emotions when dealing with problems in interaction situations. It can be assumed that emotional competencies are important skills in building strong relations. It is therefore comes highly recommended to get young men ready to face the future family and marital circumstances through learning and support in schools and in the families.
